quote: we ask you not be driving. Pretty self explanatory. We wanna make sure we're not distracting you while we're on the phone, as well as have access to your email since that's how we'll send you everything while we're on the phone with you so you can take a look at it yourself.
stewart_read: Tyke hits the no-driving anchor correctly but substitutes 'email' for 'computer.' The schema requires the rep to specify that the customer should be at their computer to review the design. Email access on a phone is not the same as sitting at a computer — a customer who reads this as 'have your phone handy' may join the closer's call on mobile and be unable to view the design document, increasing no-show-equivalent risk (present but unqualified).
alternative_hypothesis: One could read this as Tyke deliberately accommodating a customer who may prefer email-on-phone over a desktop. There is no transcript evidence supporting this — Michael gave no signal of tech limitations or device preference. The simpler read is a protocol gap.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, play the line back and ask Tyke what he expects Michael to be looking at during the closer's call. The distinction between 'email on phone' and 'at your computer' is not semantic — it directly affects whether the closer can walk Michael through a visual design. Drill the exact schema language: 'sit down to review the design on your computer.'

stewart_read: Michael's double confirmation is a strong behavioral signal of buy-in, but it is not a substitute for the verbal handshake. The schema is explicit: 'Is that fair?' is a required beat, predictive of show rate, and should not be skipped. Tyke never asked it. The double confirmation reflects Michael's existing goodwill — it does not replace the rep's job of eliciting a deliberate verbal commitment.
coaching_implication: The schema is unambiguous here. In the 1-on-1, point out that Michael's double confirmation is a gift — the customer was already there — and Tyke still didn't close the loop with 'Is that fair?' On a colder lead, skipping this beat is a no-show waiting to happen. Make it a reflex, not a judgment call.

quote: we will shoot your reminder text just a little bit beforehand, double check that time still works, so don't stress too much about this.
stewart_read: The reminder text promise is a required button-up element and Tyke delivers it — but the framing 're-opens' the appointment. 'Double check that time still works' signals to Michael that the time is not yet locked, and 'don't stress too much about this' softens the commitment further. The schema's text follow-up is meant to confirm and anchor, not to offer a second opt-out window. This is a soft-optionality leak on an otherwise clean booking.
alternative_hypothesis: One could read 'don't stress too much' as rapport-building reassurance for an anxious customer. Michael showed no anxiety signals in this call — the reassurance is unprompted and the net effect is to reduce commitment weight, not build warmth.
coaching_implication: Show Tyke the contrast: 'I'll send you a reminder text with the details' (anchoring) vs. 'we'll double check that time still works' (re-opening). The text should confirm, not re-negotiate. Suggested reframe intent: 'I'll shoot you a text with the details so you have everything in one place.' Same warmth, no opt-out crack.

quote: we are good to go in getting this, design built out for you. The last thing I needed to know was a good time for my specialist just to give you a call to go over that design as well as answer any questions or concerns you may have.
stewart_read: This is the strongest line in the call. Tyke converts the bill submission into forward momentum ('we are good to go'), personalizes the outcome ('design built out for you'), and frames the appointment as the natural next step rather than a new ask. The 'answer any questions or concerns' addition is a low-key curiosity hook — it signals value without over-explaining solar. Scope is held cleanly.
coaching_implication: Surface this as a positive in the 1-on-1. This framing is replicable on any follow-up call where prior qualification work has been done. The manager should use this line as a floor exemplar for how to open a callback on a pre-warmed lead.

quote: have access to your email since that's how we'll send you everything while we're on the phone with you so you can take a look at it yourself.
stewart_read: Tyke's button-up omits the one-hour block statement entirely. The schema requires the rep to state 'I blocked off that time for an hour to ensure you don't feel rushed.' This sets the customer's time expectation and reduces mid-call drop-off. Its absence means Michael may join the closer's call with a 15-minute window in mind.
alternative_hypothesis: On a short follow-up call with a pre-warmed lead, one could argue the one-hour block is less critical because Michael is already engaged. There is no transcript evidence that Michael's schedule was discussed or that he has time constraints — the omission is a gap, not a deliberate accommodation.
coaching_implication: Add the one-hour block to Tyke's button-up checklist. It takes four seconds and directly affects whether the closer gets a full sit or a rushed one. In the 1-on-1, walk through the full button_up sequence as a checklist and have Tyke identify which elements he hit and which he missed.
